english: cation, however, seemed not to be known to the lamas consulted, who interpreted the word: a religious gesture, the forefinger being raised, and the others drawn back. Some native dictionaries have मुष्टि fist, others खटक half-closed fist.
in C. also *a-yu* (a yu), hornless, having no horns, used of cattle Sch.
1. the short woolly hair of the yak. — 2. Lh.: venereal disease, syphilis.
Sch.: steel-yard and its weight; but Dzl. IV, 17 the word refers to an ordinary pair of scales and denotes that scale of the two which contains the weights.
corner, concave angle, nook; of rivers, lakes etc.: creek, bay, gulf, cove, also ču-k'ug (chu khug); k'ug-tu (khug tu) within a recess, on the farther side of a cavity.
a-li-k'ug-ta (a li khug ta), a kind of swallow Cs.; the lights (lungs) of this bird are used as a remedy against pulmonary diseases, Med.
and k'ug-sná (khug sna) fog, mist, haze, during a calm, esp. in spring-time.

I. sbst. 1. Cs.: "part of a long period of time" (?) — 2. a certain part of the body Med. — II. vb. 1. also k'ugs-pa (khugs pa), to call = gúg-pa (gug pa) Mil. (cf. also gyań (g.yang)). — 2. to find; get, earn; nor k'ugs-pa-ań srid (nor khugs pa ang srid) there is a possibility that we may yet replenish our cash Mil. nt.; gnyid k'ugs-pa (gnyid khugs pa) to get sleep; srań gsum k'ugs (srang gsum khugs), it drew i.e. weighed three ounces.

pouch, little bag, me-lčags-k'ug-ma (me lcags khug ma) tinder-pouch Mil.; dńul-k'ug (dngul khug) money-bag, purse; *rdzóń-k'ug* (rdzong khug) Pur. knapsack; rtsám-k'ug (rtsam khug), resp. žib-k'ug (zhib khug), little bag for flour; ńu-k'ug (ngu khug) sucking-bag, for babies.

hole, pit, hollow, cavity, originally used only of dark holes and cavities; sna-k'uń (sna khung) nostril; rna-k'uń (rna khung) the ear-hole; mčan-k'uń (mchan khung) arm-hole, arm-pit; brag-k'uń (brag khung) cleft in a rock, cavern; byi-k'uń (byi khung) mouse-hole; čab-k'uń (chab khung) a sink; bso-k'uń (bso khung) peep-hole; mda-k'uń (mda' khung) loop-hole; in C. *i-k'uń (i khung), mig-k'uń (mig khung), te-k'uń* (te khung) are used of any hole in walls, clothes etc., caused by decay or daily wear. gtor-k'uń (gtor khung) a sink, gutter; k'uń-dregs (khung dregs) soot of an oven or chimney Sch.; k'uń-pa (khung pa), k'uń-po (khung po) Cs. a large hole, k'uń-bu (khung bu) a small hole, e.g. spui k'uń-bu (spu'i khung bu) pore, passage of perspiration Dzl.

1. the original meaning perh. is mine, pit Cs. — 2. origin, source (fig.), gyoi k'uńs snubs (g.yo'i khungs snubs), he stopped the source of the deceit Ld.-Glr. Schl. 13, b. k'uńs-čan (khungs can), and prob. of similar meaning k'uńs-btsún (khungs btsun), of noble descent, or when applied to statements etc.: well founded; Cs. also fine, excellent; k'uńs-méd (khungs med), k'uńs ńán-pa (khungs ngan pa) having no 'origin', mean, pitiful, ill founded; in the last sense it seems to be used of historical accounts, Tar. 43, 5, and more esp. of religious records Pth., Glr.; gtam-k'uńs (gtam khungs) Tar. 66, 18, prob.: historical source, record, document; in Pth. facetiously: gtam-k'uńs čań yin (gtam khungs chang yin) the source of that speech is beer.

coat-lap, or any cloth serving in an emergency as a vessel; *k'u' ze'* (khu' ze') hold forth the lap of your coat, words frequently used to beggars, to whom the alms, chiefly consisting in flour, are poured into that receptacle, C.
pocket, pouch Sch.
side, edge Cs.; k'ud-du (khud du) aside, apart, secretly; k'ud-du jog-pa (khud du 'jog pa) to put, to lay aside.
or *k'yen-ti* (khyen ti), is stated to be used in Pur. for he, she.
to grunt (Sch.: to groan).
v. gum-pa (gum pa); k'ums-pa (khums pa), crooked.

Sch.: so it is said; Mil.: ló-tstsa-bai snyán-pa rgyáń-nas k'ums (lo tstsa ba'i snyan pa rgyang nas khums) might be rendered: the interpreter's renown was proclaimed from afar; the word, however, is of rare occurrence.
1. burden, load, for men, more fully: mi-k'ur (mi khur); k'ur-skyed-pas tsó-ba žig (khur skyed pas tsho ba zhig)
